title: Bargaining Under Systemic Pressure
subtitle: U.S. Objective Compression, Iranian Leverage Institutionalization, and the Reconfiguration of Negotiating Goals in the 85-Day Conflict

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher_unit: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center

alternate_identifiers.description: Policy brief number printed in the PDF

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Bargaining Under Systemic Pressure U.S. Objective Compression, Iranian Leverage Institutionalization, and the Reconfiguration of Negotiating Goals in the 85-Day Conflict.pdf

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief analyzes the first 85 days of the U.S.–Iran conflict as a shift from coercive bargaining toward procedural de-escalation. It argues that the conflict produced two parallel dynamics: U.S. objective compression and Iranian leverage institutionalization. The brief treats the reported Pakistan-mediated draft as an unconfirmed but analytically important signal of ceasefire-first sequencing rather than final settlement.
abstract: This policy brief examines how U.S. and Iranian negotiating objectives changed under cumulative systemic pressure during the first 85 days of the U.S.–Iran conflict. It argues that the conflict did not produce a clear bargaining victory for either side, but instead generated a dual structure of U.S. objective compression and Iranian leverage institutionalization. The United States shifted from coercive termination and strategic degradation of Iranian capabilities toward a narrower focus on suspending hostilities, reopening the Strait of Hormuz, managing the enriched uranium issue, and preserving an exit framework. Iran, meanwhile, expanded the bargaining package by linking settlement to sanctions relief, oil-export access, sovereignty language, security guarantees, Hormuz arrangements, and retention or controlled handling of nuclear assets. The reported Pakistan-mediated draft, if accurate, reinforces this shift by emphasizing immediate ceasefire, mutual non-targeting, freedom of navigation, joint monitoring, follow-on talks within seven days, and phased sanctions relief. The brief concludes that the negotiation became less about battlefield victory than about who could define the architecture of the post-conflict bargain.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Policy-facing assessment based on public reporting, regional media summaries, reported draft summaries, institutional and regulatory sources, and prior EPINOVA analytical frameworks. The PDF uses an appendix-based sourcing structure: Appendix A provides a chronology of reported bargaining positions and negotiation-relevant developments, while the Appendix Source List provides full source details.
methodology.case_context: The first 85 days of the U.S.–Iran conflict, with emphasis on U.S. objective compression, Iranian leverage institutionalization, the Strait of Hormuz, nuclear sequencing, sanctions relief, mediator activity, and reported Pakistan-mediated draft terms.
methodology.core_problem: The central analytical problem is why battlefield superiority and coercive pressure did not produce maximal bargaining control, and how unresolved leverage became organized through ceasefire-first sequencing and procedural stabilization.
central_claims: U.S. and Iranian negotiating objectives both changed under cumulative systemic pressure.
The United States shifted from coercive termination toward damage limitation and procedural control.
Iran shifted from wartime resistance toward bargaining-package expansion and leverage institutionalization.
The reported Pakistan-mediated draft suggests a transition toward ceasefire-first sequencing rather than final settlement.
Hormuz and the nuclear file became linked sequencing problems.
Neither side achieved its maximal objective by Day 85.
The conflict moved from maximal outcome bargaining to managed process bargaining.

analytical_lenses.name: U.S. Objective Compression
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes how systemic costs, coalition constraints, third-party exposure, and adversary-held leverage narrowed U.S. practical demands from coercive termination toward ceasefire, Hormuz reopening, nuclear-stockpile management, and procedural control.

analytical_lenses.name: Iranian Leverage Institutionalization
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes how Iran attempted to convert surviving wartime pressure tools, including Hormuz access, enriched uranium retention, sanctions bargaining, oil-export access, sovereignty language, and security guarantees, into recognized elements of a settlement structure.

analytical_lenses.name: Ceasefire-First Sequencing
analytical_lenses.description: Interprets reported draft terms as a structure in which parties suspend hostilities before resolving the most difficult substantive disputes, using monitoring, dispute-resolution procedures, follow-on talks, and conditional sanctions relief to manage unresolved leverage.

analytical_lenses.name: Hormuz Bargaining Conversion
analytical_lenses.description: Treats the Strait of Hormuz as a mechanism that converted bilateral coercive conflict into a systemic negotiation over maritime access, chokepoint governance, energy flows, and third-party exposure.

analytical_lenses.name: Nuclear Sequencing Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Separates immediate de-escalation from later negotiation over enriched uranium, enrichment limits, stockpile disposition, verification, sovereignty, and compliance architecture.

dimensions.name: U.S. Objective Compression
dimensions.observed_pattern: U.S. objectives narrowed from coercive termination and strategic degradation toward suspending hostilities, reopening Hormuz, managing enriched uranium, and preserving an exit framework.
dimensions.interpretation: Military superiority did not automatically translate into maximal settlement control.

dimensions.name: Iranian Leverage Institutionalization
dimensions.observed_pattern: Iran attempted to embed Hormuz access, nuclear sequencing, sanctions relief, sovereignty language, and security guarantees into the settlement agenda.
dimensions.interpretation: Survival under pressure became bargaining leverage.

dimensions.name: Ceasefire-First Sequencing
dimensions.observed_pattern: Reported draft terms emphasize immediate ceasefire, mutual non-targeting, freedom of navigation, joint monitoring, follow-on talks, and phased sanctions relief.
dimensions.interpretation: Procedural stabilization rather than final settlement.

dimensions.name: Hormuz Bargaining Conversion
dimensions.observed_pattern: Hormuz transformed the conflict into a negotiation over maritime access, chokepoint governance, freedom of navigation, tolling or routing claims, and third-party exposure.
dimensions.interpretation: Chokepoint leverage became central to settlement architecture.

dimensions.name: Nuclear Sequencing
dimensions.observed_pattern: The enriched-uranium and enrichment-limit issues shifted from immediate settlement to follow-on negotiation and managed handling.
dimensions.interpretation: Deferral operated as a bargaining technology rather than a substantive resolution.

dimensions.name: Sanctions as Conditional Bargaining Currency
dimensions.observed_pattern: Sanctions relief moved from a final concession to a phased and conditional exchange linked to Iranian compliance.
dimensions.interpretation: Sanctions became part of a sequenced procedural architecture.

key_findings: The first 85 days of the U.S.–Iran conflict show a shift from coercive bargaining to procedural de-escalation.
The conflict produced no clear bargaining victory for either side.
The United States retained military superiority but experienced objective compression.
Iran remained under significant pressure but preserved enough leverage to broaden the settlement agenda.
The Strait of Hormuz converted the conflict from a bilateral coercive campaign into a systemic bargaining problem.
The nuclear issue was not resolved; it was sequenced into follow-on negotiations.
The reported Pakistan-mediated draft should be treated as reported draft language, not confirmed agreement text.
The reported draft indicates ceasefire-first sequencing and procedural stabilization rather than final settlement.
The emerging framework centered on ending or suspending the war, securing Hormuz, and opening a broader negotiation window.
Military dominance may initiate coercion, but system-level leverage shapes whether coercion can be converted into settlement.
policy_recommendations: Treat Hormuz as a governance problem, not only an access problem.
Separate nuclear sequencing from nuclear abandonment.
Build ceasefire design around monitoring, reversibility, and dispute resolution.
Manage U.S.–Israeli threshold divergence when designing settlement architecture.
Treat sanctions relief as conditional sequencing leverage, not a one-time concession.
Validate alleged draft terms through official release or acknowledgement before treating them as agreement language.

watchpoints.indicator: Official release or acknowledgement of any Pakistan-mediated draft text
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Distinguishes reported draft language from confirmed agreement terms.

watchpoints.indicator: Whether follow-on talks begin within the reported seven-day window
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tests whether ceasefire-first sequencing becomes an operational negotiation track.
Tests whether Hormuz is stabilized without institutionalizing unilateral passage control.

watchpoints.indicator: Negotiation over enriched uranium stockpile handling, downblending, transfer, or verification
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tests whether nuclear sequencing produces substantive concessions or prolongs deferral.

watchpoints.indicator: Phased sanctions relief and Iranian compliance benchmarks
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether sanctions become reversible sequencing leverage or a contested concession.

watchpoints.indicator: U.S.–Israeli coordination, regional fronts, and renewed strike risk
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tests whether threshold divergence disrupts U.S.–Iran procedural stabilization.

tables.title: Early-Phase Coercive Bargaining Logic
tables.description: Reconstructs early-stage bargaining logic across U.S. and Iranian initial bargaining posture, strategic assumption, sources of leverage, desired bargaining effect, and risks.
tables.source: Author’s synthesis based on Reuters reporting on late-stage U.S.–Iran negotiations over ending the war, securing the Strait of Hormuz, and opening a broader negotiation window; supplemented by EPINOVA’s prior framework on cost-imposition dynamics in the U.S.–Iran conflict.
tables.note: Analytical reconstruction rather than a verbatim summary of any single negotiation document.

tables.title: Hormuz as Bargaining Conversion Mechanism
tables.description: Explains how maritime access, legal framing, economic impact, and negotiation role converted Hormuz into systemic bargaining leverage.
tables.source: Author’s synthesis based on The Guardian’s May 22 reporting on Qatar-mediated talks over reopening the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. opposition to Iranian tolling or PGSA-style arrangements, and Gulf-state concerns over Iranian maritime control proposals.
tables.note: Interprets Hormuz as a bargaining-conversion mechanism without classifying the legal status of Iranian or U.S. maritime actions.

tables.title: Nuclear Demand Evolution
tables.description: Tracks nuclear demand evolution from early conflict through mid-conflict, late May MOU framework, and reported final draft stage.
tables.source: Author’s synthesis based on Reuters reporting on U.S. nuclear concerns and The Guardian reporting on delayed stockpile-surrender demands and follow-on nuclear dialogue.
tables.note: Tracks sequencing of the nuclear issue rather than final resolution of nuclear obligations.

tables.title: Iranian Bargaining-Package Expansion
tables.description: Summarizes reported Iranian demand areas including cessation of hostilities, sanctions relief, oil-export restoration, blockade removal, security guarantees, compensation, nuclear sequencing, Hormuz management, and U.S. troop withdrawal.
tables.source: Author’s synthesis based on Reuters reporting on Pakistan-mediated exchanges and Iranian proposals, together with reporting from The Guardian and Al Jazeera.
tables.note: Reported bargaining positions and inferred settlement demands, not confirmed provisions of an accepted agreement.

tables.title: Reported Pakistan-Mediated Draft Terms and Strategic Meaning
tables.description: Lists reported draft terms and their strategic meaning, including immediate ceasefire, non-targeting, information-domain restraint, sovereignty language, freedom of navigation, joint monitoring, follow-on negotiations, and phased sanctions relief.
tables.source: Author’s synthesis based on secondary reporting of an alleged Pakistan-mediated draft agreement attributed to regional media reporting.
tables.note: Provisions are treated as reported draft terms rather than confirmed agreement language.

tables.title: Maximal Objectives vs. Reported Draft / Day 85 Reality
tables.description: Compares U.S. and Iranian maximal objectives with reported draft or Day 85 reality.
tables.source: Author’s comparison based on Reuters reporting that late-stage talks involved ending the war, securing the Strait of Hormuz, and opening a broader negotiation window; supplemented by reported draft terms summarized in secondary reporting.
tables.note: Maximal objectives are analytical reconstructions from public bargaining positions and reported negotiating demands.

tables.title: Compression–Expansion Bargaining Model
tables.description: Synthesizes observed bargaining movement into U.S. objective compression and Iranian leverage institutionalization across war termination, nuclear issue, Hormuz, sanctions, security guarantees, information domain, institutional mechanism, and strategic logic.
tables.source: Author’s analytical model based on public reporting from Reuters, The Guardian, Al Jazeera, and reported draft summaries; supplemented by EPINOVA’s prior conceptual work.
tables.note: Conceptual model rather than verbatim summary of a single negotiation document.

tables.title: Chronology of Phase I Developments
tables.description: Chronology of coercive onset and early Iranian resistance, Days 1–30.
tables.source: Appendix A chronology based on selected reported bargaining positions and negotiation-relevant developments.
tables.note: Selected chronology for bargaining relevance rather than comprehensive event coverage.

tables.title: Chronology of Phase II Developments
tables.description: Chronology of ceasefire proposals and nuclear-duration bargaining, Days 31–60.
tables.source: Appendix A chronology based on selected reported bargaining positions and negotiation-relevant developments.
tables.note: Selected chronology for bargaining relevance rather than comprehensive event coverage.

tables.title: Chronology of Phase III Developments
tables.description: Chronology of MOU architecture and bargaining-package expansion, Days 61–80.
tables.source: Appendix A chronology based on selected reported bargaining positions and negotiation-relevant developments.
tables.note: Selected chronology for bargaining relevance rather than comprehensive event coverage.

tables.title: Chronology of Phase IV Developments
tables.description: Chronology of ceasefire-first sequencing and reported draft terms, Days 81–85.
tables.source: Appendix A chronology based on selected reported bargaining positions and negotiation-relevant developments.
tables.note: Draft provisions are treated as reported and unconfirmed unless officially published or acknowledged by the negotiating parties or directly involved mediators.

appendices.title: Chronology of Reported Bargaining Positions, Days 1–85
appendices.description: Appendix chronology covering Phase I through Phase IV, including Tables A1–A4 and an appendix note on source limitations and draft-term uncertainty.

appendices.title: Appendix Source List
appendices.description: Reference list divided into News and Media Sources, Institutional and Regulatory Sources, and EPINOVA Sources.

funding.statement: No external funding information is provided in the PDF.

software.statement: No software artifact is specified in the PDF.

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.
